title: Minor Rider Access, Age Verification Equity, and Multilingual Community Outreach
summary: Extended discussion on minor riders: California requires ID scan by state law but New York does not; parents can enable children using their own accounts. Veo argues mandatory ID verification creates equity barriers for unbanked/undocumented riders and could push minors to unsafe unregulated e-bikes. Council proposes teen rider program (14-17) with speed-limited bikes; Veo says technically possible but requires NY state law change plus new insurance product -- 2-year horizon, parallel effort underway with City of Denver. Access program challenges: income verification friction and language barriers despite app auto-translation to phone language (Arabic specifically confirmed). Paul Kalabufo describes community outreach: partnered with Nicole Watt from Hope Speak Open for 9-language event on north side teaching New Americans to use scooters -- north side subsequently showed highest Access program usage. Veo commits to establishing equity working group with city partnership to reach riders through public housing, libraries, and community organizations.

political_signal.description: Council pressing on child safety gaps in age verification while Veo highlights equity trade-offs of mandatory ID requirements

processing_notes: IPS in detected_entities is false positive from 'tips' substring; excluded
Law Department in detected_departments is from legal context of age verification discussion; not a substantive department reference
Nicole Watt and 'Hope Speak Open' -- community partner for multilingual outreach, not in entity registry; transcription may be garbled
Denver parallel: Veo working with City of Denver on same teen rider program barriers (state law + insurance)
Segment 5 of 6 from Public Transportation Committee Meeting, Tuesday, February 4th, 2026
Transcript corrections applied: SU -> Syracuse University
